data.title: S&P 500: Picking Winners And Losers In The 20 Biggest Names
data.description:
This might be both my favorite stock screen and my favorite way to use Seeking Alpha's equity data and quant factor grades. I am a self-proclaimed Dow Industrials fan. But even I will admit that while that diverse set of 30 stocks is a great diversified shopping list for large-cap equity names, the Dow is not "the market" like it used to be.

And frankly, neither is the S&P 500. Not when most of the stocks in it don't move the needle on index performance. Here are SPY's top 10 holdings. A third of the weighting is technology, no surprise. But the 10 biggest stocks make up 36% of the index.

How skewed is it at the top? The next 10 (the 11th to 20th largest S&P 500 components) only add another 10% to the total, bringing the top 20 right around half the total index. The other 50% of the S&P 500? 450 stocks!

So this is not a market of stocks; it's a stock market that is driven by perhaps 2 dozen names. And while it's great to see some recent broadening beyond those behemoths, it is similar to the evolution of US government debt: it took so long to get here, it won't just go back in the bottle like the proverbial genie.

For those who say, "But it's always been this way," check this out. As it turns out, a more "normal" level is around 30-35% historically.

Either way, my point is that if you are trying to figure out what the market is doing, you had better know what's happening with the top 20. So let's do that.

What the 20 Biggest S&P 500 Stocks Look Like Right Now

This is a screen I maintain for quick retrieval. The iShares Top 20 US Stocks ETF (TOPT) tracks them. However, that's an ETF that has periodic rebalance dates, so it won't always be current with the top 20. For instance, since the last time I pulled this up myself, Netflix (NFLX) and AbbVie (ABBV) are out of the top 20, and Micron (MU) and Bank of America (BAC) are in. That's not some committee decision, just a run of most recent market caps (2/9/26 market close).

I'm a technician, and after decades of that as my focus, the way I use this information is as follows, as indicated with timely examples/highlights from this data:

  1. That's a quality group right there. Profitability grades are in the A category, except for JP Morgan (JPM). But that, I assume, is more about the way it is calculated. Unless they are secretly a modern Bear Stearns in disguise (yes, they did absorb that broken firm during the GFC), I'm not losing sleep over JP Morgan's statistical deficiency in that area.
  2. Valuation is what I'd expect, given my sanguine market view. F and D grades dominate. Micron scores the lone B grade, and BAC and Berkshire (BRK.B) at least land in the C range. So this is an overvalued market from a bottom-up standpoint. And remember, that translates to the full S&P 500, unless the other 480 stocks are averaging a solid B or higher. That is not the case. P/E Ratios are the opposite of slim on a trailing basis. Forward estimates are more tame, but they are estimates, not in the books yet.
  3. Growth is mixed, and that seems to be a story in this year's stock market. AI is spending, and big stocks in tech are growing. And as you can see, momentum in the stock prices follows the growth, as there's a decent correlation between the Growth and Momentum grades in the table.
  4. Note that there's an inherent bias when it comes to price momentum. Some stocks have been so big for so long, they could drop by a lot and remain in the S&P 500's top 20. Others reach the top 20 because they recently surged in price. Again, MU is a prime example
  5. The YTD performance has been spotty, as we know from watching the headlines. But what might not be as clear to many investors is how many stocks in this top 20 have really not done well the past 12 months. 8 of the 20 names are down or are up no more than 5% during that period.

BOTTOM LINE: This market is not so uniformly great or awful that we can't try to identify some potential winners and losers. So that's what I'll turn to the charts to do, projecting over a 4-6 month time frame or so.

That is, I'll use weekly charts. Daily charts are more popular, but they also tend to have too much "noise" in them, especially around earnings season, as is the case now. The weeklies hopefully smooth out the bumps and provide a clearer picture for us.

As my regular readers know, my charting style is a bit different than most. I am primarily interested in avoiding big losses along the way to lower-volatility profits. I do plenty of hedging to accomplish that. But for this article, let's pretend I don't. So I can simply take more of the type of view that I used to hear my Wall Street friends talk about a few decades ago: their job as portfolio managers was not to avoid big loss.

It was to beat the S&P 500 index using a fully invested stock portfolio. So back then, if they used sectors like Utilities (not represented among the current top 20 stocks), it was not because they loved those stocks. It was because they were not permitted by mandate to own a lot of cash or bonds or use hedging techniques.

With that specified, it's down to my favorite part, the charts. There's not enough space allotted to chart all 20. So instead I've chosen to highlight the 3 best and 3 worst I see at this moment. Earnings can swing that around a bit, but that's out of our control.

I did set out, as I normally do, to identify those in the top 20 that I estimate to have at least a 60% chance of moving up by 10% before they move down by 10%. That's my go-to return/risk tradeoff. I'd prefer 70%, but this market doesn't afford that.

Looking for Strong Stocks...looking...looking

Now, the sad part. I only found 3 that I analyze to have more than a 50% chance, based on my chart analysis. So it's a good thing I didn't promise more than that!

And as you'd expect, when looking for the "losers," those I judge to have no more than a 30% chance of gaining 10% before declining 10% from their current prices, there's a lot to choose from. In fact, 8 of the 20 stocks are what I'd call "elevated risk," whereas only 3 are "below average risk." The other 9 are less clear, which is par for the course in any analysis, technical or otherwise.

As a reminder to those less familiar with my investment process, honed over 30+ years in my former career managing private client wealth and a few mutual funds, I am very much against the traditional "buy-sell-hold" thing. Why? Because those terms are meaningless, since they only cover the WHAT portion of the portfolio decision, not the HOW MUCH aspect.

Put more simply, I can own any of these 20 stocks with 1% of my portfolio. If it fell 30% on earnings tomorrow, I would not be happy. But I would be, sort of. Because it only cost me 0.30% for every $100,000 in my portfolio, that $1,000 position would have lost $300 on that catastrophic day.

I think in "basis points of impact," not "buy or sell." Because on the flip side of that, if I own something with, say, 7% of my portfolio, and it falls by just 5% from cost, guess what? My basis point impact is 0.35%. That's $350 on every $100,000 in the portfolio.

So ratings mean very little to me without the portfolio context. I don't care about what I own individually nearly as much as I do about how my TOTAL PORTFOLIO is doing. To use a sports analogy, I care about the team winning, not who my star players are.

To bring that point home, there's an old story about a player demanding a big raise after his team finished at the bottom of the standings. The team owner's response: "We finished last with you; we can finish last without you."

I realize this is not a very popular approach in this era of "stock picks." And I'll now proceed to call out the best- and worst-looking stocks among the top 20 as I see them currently. But that context should help as you consider if you want to ask me questions about the process and analysis described here.

Charting the "Best" and "Worst" (My Way)

Now, to the charts.

Best-looking Charts Within the 20 Largest S&P 500 Stocks

Apple (AAPL)

As they say, beggars can't be choosy. Most stocks look terrible or undecided. AAPL made the top 3 out of 20, mainly on its "less bad" picture. That PPO momentum indicator at the bottom shows signs of re-accelerating. But that might be good for $10 a share, or just a few percentage points of gain. Beyond that, it might end up joining the rest of them in Mudville, so to speak.

Broadcom (AVGO)

10x forward earnings, and the quarter not being announced for another few weeks. That qualifies as relatively good news, given the messy charts I see elsewhere. So again, "less bad" makes the list here. AVGO is down 17% in 2 months. The way this market trades, that alone can be a rallying cry for the buy-the-dip cohort. The 20-day moving average just went positive for the first time since Thanksgiving time. So that's helpful.

Visa (V)

Rounding out the good list, aka the "less bad" list, here's V. Now, here's the double-edged sword on this one. $320 has been a strong "support" level. Translation: Buyers have repeatedly come in around that price. Earnings are out of the way for this quarter, having been posted in late January. So those 2 items are on the plus side.

But once again, how much upside is here? $360? $370? Not enough to make me a long-term investor in this stock at this point. I'll trade anything, but that's a whole other topic and not the focus of this article.

Worst-looking Charts Within the 20 Largest S&P 500 Stocks

One of my favorite companies (I see them a few times a day at my door, it seems) is not my favorite stock right now. I was seriously looking at it for what is, for me, a rare long-term purchase. But my analysis of the chart held me back. That was in November, around $240 a share.

Now at $204 as of this writing, I think my chances of buying it under $170 are increasing. We'll see. And if that happens, I can see collaring it at first. Then, if all goes well, loosen the hedge. That's how I roll, as they say.

Google (GOOGL)

I guess I won't be writing an article titled "best buys among the Mag-7" any time soon! GOOGL makes the list too. Microsoft (MSFT) gets honorable mention, since it is already down so much.

Here, the signs were there. What do I mean? Strong stocks become weak stocks, first by decelerating in the pace of their advance. Then, often there's a flattish period, as bulls and bears fight it out. At some point, the tug-of-war resolves itself. For GOOGL, the tipoff to me was the 20-day moving average dipping, along with the PPO. Now it's broken the 200-day moving average too. As one Wall Street pundit famously says, "Nothing good happens under the 200-day moving average." There's a lot of work to do here to stop this mini-drop from becoming a much bigger one.

Eli Lilly (LLY)

This one's for NFL fans. The irony I see in LLY making this list is that if it continues to leak (The Colts, an NFL team, play in Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, where LLY is based), this stock could go the way of the Colts season. They started out as the best team in the league and failed to make the playoffs.

LLY is so news-driven; given demand trends for its GLP-1 drug leadership, I have to listen to what the price chart is telling me. The PPO peaked in early December. And while the price resisted that technical overvaluation (higher than any point in the past 5 years on that indicator), we could look back and see this picture as the point at which the air really started to come out of the bubble. LLY has lost its $1 trillion market cap, and while any stock can go up at any time, the weight of the (technical) evidence points down here.
